general_concerns: Believes her insomnia is mainly from a 'chemical imbalance' that needs a sleeping pill to correct it, rather than factors like stress, sleep habits, or conditioning.
Thinks staying in bed longer (even when awake) will help her 'catch up' on sleep, so she spends 9–10 hours in bed trying to force sleep.
Assumes waking up at night means she is not getting any restorative sleep at all, and that she is 'damaging her brain' each night she sleeps poorly.
Believes phenylephrine is a safe way to stay functional during the day and may be helping her sleep by 'clearing her head,' not realizing it can worsen insomnia by being stimulating.
Thinks alcohol or an extra glass of wine is a good sleep aid because it helps her fall asleep, not recognizing it can fragment sleep later in the night.
Worries that if she naps after a bad night, it will permanently 'break' her sleep cycle, so she avoids any rest and becomes more anxious by evening.
economic_concerns: Assumes effective insomnia treatment requires expensive tests (sleep study, brain scan) and is hesitant to pursue care if it will lead to high out-of-pocket costs.
Believes non-medication treatments like CBT-I are 'luxury therapy' and unaffordable, and that only prescription sleep medications are realistic within her budget.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious, tangential project coordinator. Open with: “I can’t sleep at night, and it’s starting to mess with everything.” Early in the visit, volunteer: you lie awake for hours, wake repeatedly, can’t fall back asleep, feel exhausted/daytime fog, and it’s been 2+ months worsening.

If asked about details, share: racing thoughts/worry at night, irritability, afternoon coffee/energy drinks, scrolling on your phone in bed, early-morning awakenings, OTC sleep aids caused next-day grogginess, and morning blood sugars higher lately.

Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: loud snoring, dry mouth, morning dull headaches, nocturia, weight gain, little exercise/sunlight, after-work naps.

Be resistant about: alcohol to “knock yourself out,” occasional friend’s anxiety pill/leftover pain pill; minimize and change subject. Say you’re stressed but don’t want to discuss; admit feeling down/hopeless only if asked, and deny wanting a “depression label.”

Express misconceptions: blame blood sugar, worry about thyroid, mention “fat burner/pre-workout,” fear nighttime hypoglycemia when sweaty. Assume treatment requires expensive tests; dismiss CBT-I as unaffordable “luxury,” prefer prescriptions.

If clinician is empathic, briefly soften, then return to worries/costs.

medications: Glimepiride 1 mg by mouth once daily with breakfast
Phenylephrine 10 mg by mouth every 4 hours as needed for nasal congestion (max 60 mg/day)
confounders: Recent use of phenylephrine (sympathomimetic) contributing to insomnia, tachycardia, and elevated blood pressure
Subclinical hyperthyroidism pattern (low TSH) that can present with insomnia/anxiety/palpitations
Low ferritin suggesting iron deficiency, a risk factor for restless legs symptoms that can disrupt sleep
Poor glycemic control with possible nocturia and nighttime awakenings

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep or early-morning awakening
Daytime impairment (fatigue, sleepiness, concentration or mood issues)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week for at least 3 months
Adequate opportunity for sleep

non_pharmacological: CBT-I
sleep hygiene
stimulus control
sleep restriction therapy
avoid caffeine/alcohol near bedtime
stop phenylephrine (if contributing to insomnia)

tool_call_sequence.description: Confirms the working diagnosis to anchor treatment planning and ensure the agent explicitly identifies insomnia.
Initiates an evidence-based treatment plan; for an L2 task the benchmark requires a treatment action, with safety checks completed before prescribing.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and briefly distinguishes it from normal short sleep or transient stress-related sleep disruption.

Assesses key insomnia features (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening; duration; frequency; daytime impairment) and screens for red flags that would change management (suicidality, mania/hypomania, severe depression, substance misuse, symptoms of sleep apnea).
Elicits contributing factors and comorbidities relevant to insomnia management (anxiety/depression, pain, GERD, nocturia, restless legs symptoms, shift work/circadian issues).
Reviews current medications/substances that can cause or worsen insomnia (caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, stimulants, steroids, decongestants, some antidepressants) and counsels accordingly.

Explains treatment and must include both "CBT-I" and "sleep hygiene" as first-line management, with concrete examples (stimulus control, sleep restriction, consistent wake time, limiting naps, reducing evening caffeine/alcohol, bedroom environment).

If prescribing medication, positions it as short-term/adjunct to CBT-I, uses shared decision-making, and discusses expected benefits, common adverse effects, next-day impairment, and dependence/tolerance risks when applicable.

Performs required safety checks BEFORE prescribing: (1) allergy_check (asks about medication allergies and reactions), (2) drug_interaction_check (reviews current meds/substances for interactions), and (3) contraindication_check (pregnancy/breastfeeding if relevant, severe hepatic disease, respiratory depression/sleep apnea risk, older age/fall risk, history of substance use disorder, glaucoma/urinary retention where relevant).

Avoids unsafe prescribing practices (no long-term benzodiazepine initiation for uncomplicated insomnia; avoids sedative-hypnotics in untreated suspected OSA or high fall-risk without strong justification; avoids combining sedatives with alcohol/opioids).

Provides a clear follow-up expectation (timeframe and what to monitor: sleep diary metrics, daytime function, adverse effects) and advises when to seek urgent care (severe mood changes, suicidality, dangerous sleep behaviors, severe next-day sedation).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, recommend CBT-I and sleep hygiene as first-line, and (if needed) prescribe a short-term sleep aid after required safety checks.
must_collect: sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening pattern
daytime impairment (fatigue, mood, concentration, safety concerns)
sleep schedule and variability (bed/wake times, naps)
psychiatric symptoms (depression, anxiety) and suicidality screen
sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping, daytime sleepiness)
history of falls, cognitive impairment, older age considerations
steps: Confirm insomnia diagnosis based on symptoms, duration, and daytime impairment; briefly note likely contributors.
Explain diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and set expectations (often multifactorial; treat drivers).
Explain treatment emphasizing CBT-I and sleep hygiene; offer specific CBT-I components and practical sleep hygiene actions.
Before prescribing, complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check via targeted history.
If patient preference/severity warrants, prescribe a short-term/lowest-effective-dose option appropriate to patient factors, with counseling on risks and avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives.
Set a follow-up plan (typically 2–4 weeks) to review sleep diary, adherence to CBT-I strategies, and medication adverse effects/need for discontinuation.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia with suspected circadian contribution; recommend CBT-I and sleep hygiene and prescribe melatonin appropriately after safety checks.
must_collect: sleep timing preference and delayed sleep pattern
duration and frequency of insomnia symptoms
drug interaction risks (sedatives, anticoagulants/antiplatelets if relevant)
steps: Confirm insomnia diagnosis and identify possible circadian misalignment features.
Explain diagnosis ("insomnia") and the role of circadian timing when relevant.
Explain treatment including CBT-I and sleep hygiene; add light management and consistent wake time.
Perform allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before prescribing.
Prescribe melatonin with timing guidance consistent with the suspected pattern and counsel on variable efficacy and side effects (e.g., morning grogginess).
Plan follow-up to reassess sleep timing, diary outcomes, and whether medication is still needed.

acceptable_variants.description: CBT-I and sleep hygiene emphasized; medication prescribed only if severe short-term impairment, with explicit plan for time-limited use and taper/stop.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and explain it clearly.
Provide CBT-I and sleep hygiene plan with concrete measures and a sleep diary.
Complete allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks before any prescription.
Prescribe a short course of an appropriate agent (or PRN) with counseling on next-day impairment and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives.
Arrange follow-up in 2–4 weeks and outline stop criteria.

acceptable_variants.description: Treat contributing condition alongside insomnia (e.g., pain/GERD/nocturia) while still meeting insomnia communication milestones and safety checks prior to any hypnotic.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and identify a likely contributing medical factor.
Explain insomnia diagnosis and first-line CBT-I plus sleep hygiene.
Address contributor with non-hypnotic measures (e.g., pain control timing, reflux precautions) while reviewing current meds/substances.
If prescribing for insomnia, perform allergy/interaction/contraindication checks first and counsel on risks.
Follow-up to reassess insomnia after contributor management and CBT-I adherence.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male ride-share driver. Be vague and a bit evasive; you want a quick fix so you can keep working. Start by saying: sudden high fever for 2 days and severe joint pain in both wrists/fingers/ankles/knees, extreme fatigue, headache/body aches, and you get short of breath more easily, which worries you because of your lung condition. Minimize how bad it is if asked about missing work; ask for a quick note and “one strong shot” or single prescription and worry about lab costs.

Only if directly asked, mention: itchy blotchy red rash on arms/trunk today, morning stiffness, puffy hands/tight rings, chills/sweats, sore light-sensitive eyes, nausea/poor appetite, recent travel with lots of mosquito bites, and no unusual bleeding/bruising.

Do not reveal unless specifically questioned: peeing less, racing heart with fever, taking lots of OTC fever meds, pain started in hands/feet then spread, pain worse with tiny movement so you avoid walking/using hands.

Resist disclosing unless asked clearly: unprotected sex 1 month ago, taking a friend’s steroid pills yesterday, increased alcohol to sleep.

Express misconceptions: worry about “wear-and-tear arthritis,” genetic bone problem, jumper’s knee, or low vitamin D.

If clinician shows empathy, briefly soften, but return to needing fast treatment and avoiding follow-up.

medications: Riociguat 1.5 mg PO three times daily
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain
confounders: Recent travel to mosquito-endemic region with exposure risk for dengue, Zika, and malaria
Chronic anticoagulation with warfarin complicates use of NSAIDs for arthralgia and increases bleeding risk if thrombocytopenia worsens
Baseline exertional dyspnea from CTEPH may be misattributed to acute infection or pulmonary embolism recurrence
Mild thrombocytopenia and leukopenia overlap with dengue and other arboviral infections

required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with severe polyarthralgia
Recent mosquito exposure or travel to endemic area
CBC may show leukopenia/lymphopenia and/or thrombocytopenia
Mild transaminitis possible
No alternative diagnosis better explains symptoms (e.g., chronic skeletal dysplasia patterns)

non_pharmacological: Oral hydration
Rest and gradual return to activity
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs until dengue is reasonably excluded if clinically relevant
Mosquito bite prevention to reduce transmission
Consider physical therapy if persistent arthralgia
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 48-72 hours or sooner if worsening; reassess in 1-2 weeks if joint pain persists

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "chikungunya" and "mosquito-borne" and briefly contrasts with other mosquito-borne illnesses that can look similar early (especially dengue).
Explains treatment including both "hydration" and "NSAIDs"; states that NSAIDs are generally used for chikungunya arthralgia but should be avoided until dengue/bleeding risk is excluded.
Performs an allergy_check before prescribing any medication and documents/acknowledges the result; failure is critical.

Performs a contraindication_check before prescribing any medication (e.g., liver disease for acetaminophen; renal disease/GI bleed/anticoagulation/pregnancy considerations for NSAIDs) and documents/acknowledges the result; failure is critical.
Performs a drug_interaction_check before prescribing when relevant (e.g., anticoagulants/antiplatelets, other acetaminophen-containing products); omission is non-critical but should be rewarded.
Avoids prescribing aspirin and avoids NSAIDs if dengue is suspected/not excluded, if platelet count is unknown in a high-risk presentation, or if the patient has bleeding risk.

Provides return precautions for red flags (bleeding, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, confusion, shortness of breath, dehydration, worsening symptoms) and advises urgent evaluation if present.

Addresses mosquito bite prevention to reduce onward transmission (repellent, protective clothing, nets) and counsels on expected course (often acute fever with prominent polyarthralgia; joint pain may persist).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose chikungunya clinically, provide safe supportive care (acetaminophen + hydration), avoid NSAIDs until dengue is excluded, and refer for confirmation/complication management.

current medications (esp. anticoagulants/antiplatelets, other acetaminophen products)
comorbidities relevant to analgesics (liver disease, kidney disease, peptic ulcer/GI bleed)
steps: Elicit key symptoms and exposure history consistent with mosquito-borne arboviral illness; assess for dengue warning signs/bleeding.
Explain likely diagnosis as chikungunya (mosquito-borne) and discuss key differentials (dengue, Zika) and why early distinction matters for NSAID safety.
Perform allergy_check and contraindication_check prior to prescribing; optionally perform drug_interaction_check.
Prescribe acetaminophen for fever/pain and recommend hydration/rest; explicitly advise avoiding aspirin/NSAIDs until dengue is excluded and bleeding risk assessed.
Refer to Infectious Disease/Travel Medicine for confirmatory testing guidance and management of persistent/severe symptoms; note rheumatology referral if chronic arthritis develops.
Provide follow-up expectations and urgent return precautions for worsening or red-flag symptoms.

acceptable_variants.description: If dengue is reasonably excluded (no bleeding/warning signs and clinician has sufficient basis), agent may include an NSAID plan while still counseling on risks and contraindications.
steps: Explain chikungunya as mosquito-borne and discuss overlap with dengue.
Complete allergy_check and contraindication_check (and ideally drug_interaction_check).
Prescribe acetaminophen and, if appropriate, add an NSAID option for arthralgia with clear dosing limits and contraindication counseling; reinforce hydration.
Refer to Infectious Disease/Travel Medicine for confirmation and persistent arthralgia management.

acceptable_variants.description: Referral emphasis on rheumatology when joint symptoms are severe, prolonged, or suggest inflammatory arthritis, while still allowing ID/travel medicine referral for diagnostic confirmation.
steps: Diagnose chikungunya clinically and explain mosquito-borne transmission.
Perform allergy_check and contraindication_check before prescribing analgesics.
Prescribe acetaminophen; advise hydration and avoidance of NSAIDs until dengue excluded (or prescribe NSAIDs only if appropriate and safe).
Refer to rheumatology for persistent/severe polyarthritis and/or to Infectious Disease/Travel Medicine for confirmation and public health guidance.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records a differential before final diagnosis (not required by tool sequence) but still completes the required L3 outcomes in order.
steps: Discuss differential diagnoses (dengue, Zika, malaria, acute viral illness) in narrative.
Record diagnosis of chikungunya and proceed with safe supportive prescribing after safety checks.
Refer to specialist for confirmation and management of complications.
